kernel_variant.question:
Let m and n be integers with m,n \ge 4.  Consider the (2m-1) \times (2n-1) rectangle whose sides are parallel to the coordinate axes and which is subdivided into unit squares.  Two kinds of tiles may be used (rotations and reflections are allowed throughout):

*  Type A (triomino).  Begin with a 2 \times 2 block of unit squares and delete one of the four squares.  The remaining three unit squares form an L-shaped triomino.

*  Type B (tetromino).  Begin with the 2 \times 3 block whose lower-left square has coordinates (0,0).  Delete the squares (0,1) and (2,0); the four remaining squares (0,0),(1,0),(1,1),(2,1) form a zig-shaped tetromino.  Any rotation or reflection of this shape is also allowed.

Each Type A tile therefore covers exactly 3 unit squares, and each Type B tile covers exactly 4 unit squares.

Determine, as a function of m and n, the minimum possible number of tiles that can tile the entire (2m-1) \times (2n-1) rectangle without gaps or overlaps.
kernel_variant.solution:
Answer:  \boxed{mn}.

1.  A lower bound

   Label the unit squares of the big rectangle by their integer coordinates (i,j) with 1\le i\le 2m-1 and 1\le j\le 2n-1.  Colour square (i,j) red exactly when both i and j are odd.  The red squares form an m \times n array of isolated cells, so there are mn red squares in total.

   We now show that every admissible tile---of either type---meets at most one red square.  This will imply that at least mn tiles are necessary.

   *  Type A.  A Type A triomino is contained in a 2 \times 2 block.  Such a block contains exactly one red square because precisely one of the four ordered pairs (even,even), (even,odd), (odd,even), (odd,odd) has both coordinates odd.  Hence a Type A tile meets at most one red square.

   *  Type B.  Fix an orientation of the tetromino and denote the four occupied squares, in local coordinates, by (x_1,y_1),\ldots ,(x_4,y_4).  These coordinates, taken modulo 2, are always the same multiset \{(0,0),(1,0),(1,1),(0,1)\}.  Consequently, exactly one of the four local squares has both coordinates odd.  Translating the shape by an integral vector (a,b) just adds (a,b) to each coordinate, so still exactly one of the four global squares has both coordinates odd.  Thus every Type B tile contains precisely one red square and therefore cannot contain two.

   Combining the two bullets, no tile covers more than one red square, so at least mn tiles are required.

2.  A useful lemma

   Lemma.  For every integer k \ge 3, a 2 \times (2k-1) rectangle can be tiled with exactly k tiles: two of Type A and k-2 of Type B.

   Proof.  Place a Type A triomino in the lower-left corner so that it covers squares (1,1),(1,2),(2,2).  For each j with 1 \le j \le k-2 place one Type B tile whose leftmost column is 2j; concretely it covers the squares (2j,1),(2j+1,1),(2j+1,2),(2j+2,2).  The remaining three uncovered squares (2k-2,1),(2k-1,1),(2k-1,2) form an L-shape congruent to Type A, and one more Type A tile finishes the tiling. \blacksquare 

3.  A 7 \times 7 square can be tiled with exactly 16 tiles

   Let t be the number of Type A tiles and s the number of Type B tiles in any tiling of a 7 \times 7 board.  The area equation is
        3t + 4s = 49.                        (1)
   From Section 1 we already know that t + s \ge 16.                (2)

   Subtract 3\cdot (2) from (1):
        (3t + 4s) - 3(t + s) = 49 - 48
        \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;   s = 1.
   Inserting s = 1 into (1) gives t = 15.  Hence every tiling of the 7 \times 7 board must use exactly 15 triominoes and 1 tetromino.

   An explicit arrangement with these counts is listed below (coordinates are (column,row) with (1,1) in the lower-left corner).

   *  Type B tile (the unique tetromino)
        B : (2,2),(3,2),(3,3),(4,3).

   *  Fifteen Type A tiles (each line lists the three occupied squares)
        A_1 : (1,1),(1,2),(2,1)
        A_2 : (3,1),(4,1),(4,2)
        A_3 : (5,1),(5,2),(6,1)
        A_4 : (6,2),(7,1),(7,2)
        A_5 : (1,3),(2,3),(1,4)
        A_6 : (5,3),(5,4),(6,3)
        A_7 : (6,4),(7,3),(7,4)
        A_8 : (3,4),(3,5),(4,4)
        A_9 : (2,4),(1,5),(2,5)
        A_{10}: (4,5),(5,5),(5,6)
        A_{11}: (6,5),(7,5),(7,6)
        A_{12}: (1,6),(1,7),(2,6)
        A_{13}: (2,7),(3,6),(3,7)
        A_{14}: (4,6),(4,7),(5,7)
        A_{15}: (6,6),(6,7),(7,7).

   One checks directly that the above 49 squares are pairwise distinct and fill the board, so the required tiling exists.

4.  Tiling the general (2m-1) \times (2n-1) rectangle

   Place the above 7 \times 7 tiling in the lower-left corner of the big rectangle.

   *  To the right of it remains a 7 \times (2m-8) rectangle.  Divide it into m-4 vertical strips of size 7 \times 2.  After a 90^\circ rotation each strip becomes 2 \times 7, and by the lemma (with k = 4) each strip is tiled by exactly 4 tiles.  Total here: 4(m-4) tiles.

   *  Above the 7-row band remains a (2n-8) \times (2m-1) rectangle---that is, n-4 horizontal strips, each of size 2 \times (2m-1).  By the lemma (with k = m) each such strip is tiled with m tiles.  Total here: m(n-4) tiles.

   Summing gives
            16   (for the 7 \times 7 square)
          + 4(m-4)
          + m(n-4)
          = mn.

   This equals the lower bound found in Section 1, so mn is indeed the minimum possible number of tiles.

Therefore the least number of tiles required to tile the (2m-1) \times (2n-1) rectangle is exactly mn.

core_steps: Parity-color all unit squares with both coordinates odd; each tile meets at most one such square ⇒ ≥ mn tiles are necessary.
Lemma: any 2 × (2k−1) strip (k≥3) can be covered with k tiles.
Base case: construct an explicit tiling of one (2p−1) × (2p−1) square (here p=4, side 7) with T tiles (here T=16).
Partition the large board into that base square, (m−p) horizontal 2 × (2p−1) strips, and (n−p) vertical (2m−1) × 2 strips.
Tile each piece with the previous lemmas; tile counts add to mn, attaining the lower bound.

slot_p.description: Chosen ‘pivot’ size p for the base (2p−1)×(2p−1) block; all later counts use this same p.

slot_base_side.description: Side length of the base square, equal to 2·slot_p−1.

slot_T.description: Number of tiles used to cover the base square.

slot_k_min.description: Smallest k for which the 2×(2k−1) strip-tiling lemma is invoked.

slot_coeff_p.description: The coefficient appearing in (m−p) and (n−p) when counting strips (identical to slot_p).
